quick_facts.label: Role focus
quick_facts.value: A Design Researcher investigates users’ needs, behaviours, workflows and pain points, then turns evidence into insights that product and design teams can use.

quick_facts.label: Common research activities
quick_facts.value: Current India-based UX research work can include planning and running user interviews, conducting usability tests, and synthesising findings into clear, actionable insights.

quick_facts.label: Relevant postgraduate preparation
quick_facts.value: M.Des pathways relevant to this role include Interaction Design, Product Design and research-focused design programmes. NID lists Interaction Design and Product Design among its M.Des specialisations; IDC IIT Bombay offers Interaction Design and M.Des by Research.

quick_facts.label: Research-methods learning
quick_facts.value: IDC IIT Bombay’s Interaction Design curriculum includes User Studies, Design Evaluation, Quantitative Design Research Methods, Qualitative Design Research Methods and a Design Research Seminar.

quick_facts.label: Work setting example
quick_facts.value: The role may involve field or remote research with domain stakeholders and close collaboration with product and design teams; an AICTE-listed Bengaluru internship, for example, involves research with clinicians and expecting mothers for clinical-platform and consumer-app work.

sections.heading: What a Design Researcher does
paragraphs: A Design Researcher frames a research question around a product, service or experience; selects appropriate methods; gathers evidence from people and contexts; analyses the evidence; and communicates implications for design decisions. In an AICTE-listed UX Researcher internship in Bengaluru, the stated work includes planning and conducting interviews with healthcare stakeholders, usability testing a B2B clinical platform and B2C app, and synthesising findings for product and design teams.

The exact remit varies by employer and domain. A Delhi Design Researcher posting describes stakeholder observation, interviews, surveys, contextual inquiry, process and touchpoint mapping, documentation of operations, and synthesis of qualitative and quantitative data into recommendations for product, service and platform design.
bullets: Plan studies: define the decision to inform, participant profiles, methods, discussion guides or test tasks, and logistics.
Conduct generative and evaluative research, such as interviews, contextual observation, surveys and usability testing.
Analyse and synthesise evidence into themes, opportunity areas, personas, journeys, workflows or recommendations.
Present clear findings and collaborate with designers, product managers, engineers and other stakeholders so research influences subsequent work.

sections.heading: Entry routes in India
paragraphs: There is no single named degree route demonstrated by the sources. A practical route is an undergraduate design or related degree followed by an M.Des in a relevant specialisation, while another is to build research capability through projects, internships and a portfolio that demonstrates rigorous research practice.

For formal postgraduate study, NID’s M.Des is a two-and-a-half-year programme with specialisations that include Product Design and Interaction Design; admission is based on the two-stage Design Aptitude Test (DAT). IDC IIT Bombay offers M.Des programmes including Interaction Design, and its curriculum explicitly includes user studies and qualitative and quantitative design-research methods.

Students from non-design undergraduate backgrounds can also find M.Des entry options: NIFT states that its M.Des eligibility includes an undergraduate degree in any discipline from a recognised institution, subject to the applicable admission process and prospectus.
bullets: Build foundational capability through B.Des or through another undergraduate discipline combined with demonstrable user-research and design work.
Consider M.Des specialisations such as Interaction Design or Product Design where the course content and projects include user-centred and research-oriented work.
Use internships, live projects and case studies to show research planning, ethical participant engagement, analysis, synthesis and influence on a design decision.

For a research-intensive route, IDC IIT Bombay’s M.Des by Research requires a research proposal; shortlisted candidates are assessed through a written test and interview for design ability, research aptitude and topic knowledge.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: Strong preparation combines research craft with design literacy. The IIT Bombay Interaction Design curriculum identifies user studies, design evaluation, quantitative and qualitative design-research methods, human factors and communication-oriented work as relevant areas of learning. Employer and internship descriptions additionally show the importance of conducting interviews and usability tests, analysing evidence, and producing actionable outputs for cross-functional teams.

A credible early-career portfolio should therefore show not only polished artefacts, but the research decision being addressed, recruitment rationale, method choice, evidence trail, synthesis process, limitations and the concrete action or design change informed by the study.
bullets: Qualitative methods: interview planning and moderation, contextual inquiry or field observation, note-taking, coding and thematic synthesis.
Evaluative methods: usability-test planning, task design, observation and prioritisation of findings.
Quantitative literacy: survey design, careful interpretation of data and understanding when quantitative evidence is appropriate.
Synthesis and communication: journey or process mapping, insight framing, written reporting, visual storytelling and stakeholder presentations.
Collaboration and judgement: working with product, design and engineering colleagues; matching the research method to the decision, user context and constraints.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is done
paragraphs: Design research can sit in product companies, design consultancies, technology teams and domain-specific organisations. The work may be conducted in the field, on-site, remotely, or through a combination of these modes. The AICTE-listed Bengaluru role is tied to healthcare products and calls for interviews with clinicians and expecting mothers, while a Delhi role describes on-site and remote stakeholder immersions to understand workflows and behaviour.

The role is inherently cross-functional: research findings are prepared to guide product and design work, and postings identify collaboration with product managers, designers, engineers and stakeholders as part of the job.
bullets: Digital-product contexts may involve research on web platforms, mobile apps and internal tools.
Service and domain contexts may require observing real-world workflows and speaking with multiple stakeholder groups.
Research activity often alternates between participant-facing work, analysis and synthesis, and alignment sessions with delivery teams.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: Progression is not standardised across Indian employers, but available postings illustrate a move from supervised or internship-level research work toward independently planning and leading studies. The AICTE internship describes hands-on interview, testing and synthesis work; a senior Design Researcher posting in Pune asks for three or more years of experience and capability in converting a client brief into a research brief, planning studies, recruiting interview profiles, creating discussion guides, and producing research artefacts such as personas and journey maps.

A researcher can deepen into specialist UX, ethnographic or service-design research; become a senior or lead researcher responsible for research strategy and practice; or move into adjacent roles such as design strategy, product design or research management. The appropriate direction depends on the person’s portfolio, sector knowledge and appetite for hands-on research versus team or programme leadership.
bullets: Early stage: support interviews, testing, documentation, analysis and research operations while building a portfolio of end-to-end studies.
Mid stage: independently scope and run studies, select methods, synthesise evidence and influence multiple product or service decisions.
Senior stage: shape research strategy, guide stakeholders and teams, improve research practice and mentor other researchers.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation is highly variable by employer, city, sector, seniority, employment type and whether a role is an internship or a permanent position. As a dated example rather than a market benchmark, an AICTE-listed full-time UX Researcher internship in Bengaluru displayed a stipend of INR 15,000–20,000 per month and a six-month duration; its application deadline was July 4, 2026. This figure should not be treated as a salary expectation for Design Researcher roles generally.

Candidates should assess an offer as a whole: scope of research ownership, access to mentorship, participant-recruitment support, opportunities to work across discovery and evaluative research, portfolio value, location or remote-work terms, and total compensation. Verify current pay and terms directly with the employer because listings and conditions can change.
bullets: Use current employer postings and direct recruiter discussions to validate compensation for the specific location and level.
Distinguish internship stipends from annual compensation for permanent roles.
Compare role scope and learning opportunity alongside pay, especially at entry level.
